name: Other fermented milk (food medium)
basis: per 100 g (approx. per 100 mL; density ~1.03 g/mL) of plain unsweetened fermented/cultured milk (soured milk / kefir / cultured buttermilk type)

components.name: lactic acid
components.source: Kim et al. 2019 commercial drinking yogurt survey, PMC6837898 (lactate mean 767.67 mg/100g); FIL/IDF & Codex fermented-milk standard (0.6-1.8% titratable acidity as lactic acid)
components.evidence: Dominant fermentation product and a primary carbon/energy source for lactate-utilising microbes; representative 0.8 g/100g, range 0.6-1.8 depending on ripening/acidity (pH 3.8-4.6).

components.name: citric acid
components.source: Kim et al. 2019, PMC6837898 (citrate mean 170.91 mg/100g, range 137-215)
components.evidence: Native milk organic acid, partially retained after fermentation; co-metabolised with lactose by citrate-positive lactococci to diacetyl/CO2.

components.name: acetic acid
components.source: Kim et al. 2019, PMC6837898 (acetate mean 31.92 mg/100g, range 18-43)
components.evidence: Heterofermentative/citrate co-product; growth-relevant weak acid and carbon source. Kefir/ropy milks trend to the higher end.

components.name: lactose
components.source: Alm 1982 J Dairy Sci 65:346 doi:10.3168/jds.S0022-0302(82)82198-X (buttermilk/kefir/ropy milk 20-30% lactose reduction from 4.8 g/100g); USDA FDC 171256 cultured lowfat buttermilk (total sugars 5.4 g)
components.evidence: Residual disaccharide after partial fermentation; still the largest single sugar pool and the main primary carbon source. Range ~3.4-4.4 g/100g.

components.name: D-galactose
components.source: Adhikari/Nilsson (Lund) 2017 Int Dairy J doi:10.1016/j.idairyj.2017.06.005; Alm 1982 doi:10.3168/jds.S0022-0302(82)82198-X (free galactose rises from traces to ~1.3 g/100g in yogurt)
components.evidence: Free monosaccharide released by beta-galactosidase and typically NOT consumed by Gal-negative starters, so it accumulates; key growth-relevant free sugar. Range ~0.5-1.3.

components.name: D-glucose
components.source: Adhikari/Nilsson 2017 Int Dairy J doi:10.1016/j.idairyj.2017.06.005; Kim et al. 2019 PMC6837898 (glucose variable, largely consumed in plain product)
components.evidence: Glucose moiety liberated from lactose is preferentially fermented, so only a small residual pool remains in plain fermented milk (higher only where sugar/ingredients are added).

components.name: total protein
components.evidence: Casein + whey; substrate for proteolytic release of free amino acids/peptides during fermentation.

components.name: L-glutamate (+ glutamine)
components.evidence: Most abundant protein-derived amino acid; free-glutamate fraction rises with proteolysis.

components.name: L-proline
components.evidence: Casein-rich residue.

components.name: L-leucine
components.evidence: Branched-chain, growth-essential for many LAB.

components.name: total fat
components.evidence: Representative lowfat cultured product; full-fat fermented milks run ~3.3 g/100g.

components.name: palmitic acid (C16:0)
components.evidence: Dominant saturated milk fatty acid.

components.name: oleic acid (C18:1 n-9)
components.evidence: Principal monounsaturated milk fatty acid.

components.name: butyric acid (C4:0)
components.evidence: Characteristic short-chain milk fatty acid.

components.name: cholesterol
components.evidence: Genuine constituent of dairy fat (kept - not implausible in an animal food).

components.name: calcium
components.evidence: Defining dairy mineral.

components.name: iron
components.evidence: Milk is genuinely iron-poor.

components.name: manganese
components.evidence: Trace.

components.name: iodine
components.source: McCance & Widdowson / EFSA dairy iodine review (milk 15-40 ug/100g)
components.evidence: Milk/dairy is a leading dietary iodine source; genuinely characteristic of this matrix. Range 15-40 depending on feed/season.

components.name: riboflavin (B2)
components.evidence: Dairy is a leading riboflavin source; some LAB also synthesise it.

components.name: biotin (B7)
components.evidence: Growth-essential cofactor for many LAB; USDA panel omits it but dairy is a genuine source.

components.name: folate (B9)
components.evidence: Can rise in fermented milks where starters synthesise folate.

components.name: vitamin B12 (cobalamin)
components.evidence: Delivers the biologically relevant cobalt (free Co2+ ion removed as noise).

components.name: vitamin C (ascorbate)
components.evidence: Low; native to milk.

components.name: vitamin K1 (phylloquinone)
components.evidence: Trace fat-associated vitamin.

components.name: water
components.evidence: Bulk solvent of the medium.

remove.name: (R)-Oxypeucedanin
remove.reason: Plant furanocoumarin (from Apiaceae); biologically implausible in fermented milk - FooDB predicted/expected, not measured

remove.name: epsilon-Polylysine
remove.reason: Manufactured microbial-derived preservative additive, not a native nutrient of fermented milk

remove.name: (E)-2-Phenyl-2-butenal
remove.reason: Trace aroma volatile, not a quantitatively meaningful growth nutrient

remove.name: (±)-erythro-Isoleucine
remove.reason: Stereoisomer artifact / duplicate of L-isoleucine; not a distinct nutrient pool

remove.name: Nonanoate (nonanoic acid, C9:0)
remove.reason: Odd-chain C9 acid is not a characteristic milk fatty acid at nutrient level; FooDB trace/noise

remove.name: Co2+
remove.reason: Free cobalt ion is a non-nutritive ultratrace; biologically relevant cobalt is supplied by vitamin B12

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: Non-nutritive trace contaminant, not a growth nutrient of milk

remove.name: Chromium
remove.reason: Non-nutritive ultratrace element, not characteristic of the milk matrix

missing_added: lactic acid (dominant fermentation product - was entirely absent)
lactose with a quantitative residual amount
keep_uncovered: beta-Lactose (anomer of lactose; same metabolite, no separate BiGG met)
Myristoleic acid (C14:1; genuine minor milk fatty acid, no priority BiGG met)
Gadoleic acid (C20:1; genuine trace milk fatty acid)
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 171256 - Milk, buttermilk, fluid, cultured, lowfat (NDB 01088), full nutrient/amino-acid/fatty-acid panel
Kim et al. 2019, Comprehensive Evaluation of Commercial Drinking Yogurts in Korea, PMC6837898 - organic acids (lactate/citrate/acetate) and residual sugars
Adhikari & Nilsson (Lund University) 2017, Lactose, glucose and galactose content in milk, fermented milk and lactose-free milk products, Int Dairy J doi:10.1016/j.idairyj.2017.06.005
Alm 1982, Effect of Fermentation on Lactose, Glucose, and Galactose Content in Milk, J Dairy Sci 65:346 doi:10.3168/jds.S0022-0302(82)82198-X
McCance & Widdowson / EFSA dairy references - iodine and biotin content
notes: Dominant carbon/energy sources are lactic acid (the defining fermentation product, ~0.6-1.8 g/100g) plus residual lactose (~4 g/100g) and accumulating free D-galactose (~1 g/100g); free glucose is largely consumed. The database record lacked ALL organic acids and any specific-sugar breakdown - these were the main gaps and were added quantitatively. Values represent a plain unsweetened fermented milk (soured milk/kefir/cultured-buttermilk type); sweetened/flavoured drinking yogurts carry added sucrose/fructose not included here. FooDB noise (a plant furanocoumarin, a preservative additive, free Co2+/Ni/Cr trace ions, C9 fatty acid) removed.
